meta.certificationName: Microsoft Certified: Azure AI Apps and Agents Developer Associate (AI-103)
meta.title: MS-AI103-5: Agent Orchestration, Retrieval, and Safety Controls

questions.stem: A product team wants to implement a generative AI feature where the chatbot proactively summarizes the conversation every 5 turns to stay within the context window while preserving key information. What is the main trade-off of this approach?

options.text: Summarization adds latency from the additional LLM call, and the summary may lose specific details that are needed to answer follow-up questions
Summarization permanently fine-tunes the model on the conversation, causing it to forget its original training
Summarization prevents the use of retrieval-augmented generation in subsequent turns
Summarization increases the context window requirement, making the approach counterproductive

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because rolling summarization introduces two costs: (1) latency from the additional LLM call needed to generate the summary, and (2) information loss — a summary compresses detail, and specific facts from earlier in the conversation may be omitted yet become relevant in later turns.

**B** is incorrect because LLM inference does not modify model weights — summarization is just another inference call.

**C** is incorrect because RAG retrieval operates independently of how conversation history is managed.

**D** is incorrect because summarization reduces, not increases, the amount of context being maintained — that is precisely the goal.

[Conversation history management in Azure OpenAI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/how-to/chatgpt)

questions.stem: One team is designing a RAG system for a technical knowledge base. Select TWO factors that most directly affect the quality of embedding-based retrieval. (Select TWO)

options.text: The temperature parameter of the generator LLM
The number of GPU cores on the vector search server
The chunking strategy and chunk size used during document ingestion
The embedding model used to encode queries and documents

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the embedding model determines the quality of vector representations — a domain-relevant model produces better representations than a generic one, directly impacting retrieval relevance.

**C** is correct because chunk size and strategy directly affect what information is in each retrievable unit — too small loses context, too large reduces precision.

**B** is incorrect because GPU cores affect search latency and throughput but not the semantic quality of retrieval results.

**D** is incorrect because the generator's temperature controls output randomness and has no effect on the retrieval step.

[Azure AI Search vector search overview](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/vector-search-overview)

questions.stem: A deployment is underperforming because the wrong approach was chosen. If the real requirement is **reliably constrain assistant behavior with durable top-priority instructions**, which correction is BEST?

options.text: Put the governing instructions in the system message
Place all behavior rules only in user messages
Hope the model infers the intended policy from retrieved documents
Use embeddings instead of prompt instructions

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because the system message is the primary place to establish durable role, policy, and formatting constraints. **B** is incorrect because user messages are lower-trust and easier to conflict with later prompts **C** is incorrect because retrieved context is not a strong substitute for explicit instructions **D** is incorrect because embeddings help retrieval, not instruction hierarchy
questions.reference: [How to use function calling with Azure OpenAI in Microsoft Foundry Models - Microsoft Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/how-to/function-calling)

questions.stem: A support agent must remember important user preferences across separate sessions days apart. Which memory design is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Keep all historical turns in the active prompt indefinitely
Use a larger Azure OpenAI model with a bigger context window
Persist long-term memory outside the immediate chat window in an external store and retrieve it when relevant at the start of each session
Rely only on the model's parametric knowledge from pretraining

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because cross-session memory requires external persistence (e.g., Azure Cosmos DB or Azure AI Search) and selective retrieval at the start of each new session. This is the only approach that survives the end of a chat context.

**B** is incorrect because keeping all historical turns in-context is expensive and eventually impossible once accumulated history exceeds the context window limit.

**C** is incorrect because the model's pretraining knowledge does not include user-specific preferences or conversation history.

**D** is incorrect because even a very large context window does not provide cross-session persistence — the context is reset at the start of each new session.

[Microsoft Foundry Agent Service overview](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/foundry/agents/overview)

questions.stem: A project lead wants to use the managed platform capability that best supports **preserve important history in a long-running assistant conversation without overflowing the model context**. What should the team choose?

options.text: Replace the system prompt with the conversation history
Increase temperature to make the model remember more
Summarize older turns and keep the latest high-value details in context
Append every previous message forever

questions.explanation: **C** is correct because conversation summarization compresses older context while preserving salient facts within the available context window. **A** is incorrect because removing system guidance weakens control and still does not solve growth of history **B** is incorrect because temperature does not expand context capacity **D** is incorrect because unbounded history eventually exceeds context limits

questions.stem: One team is planning a solution and needs to **let a model call approved business functions with validated argument schemas**. Which service or design choice should they make?

options.text: Store tool instructions inside retrieved documents and hope the model follows them
Fine-tune the model instead of using structured tool definitions
Ask the model to describe the desired API call in plain text and let the user interpret it
Define tools/functions with JSON-schema-style parameters and execute them only when the model requests a tool call

questions.explanation: **D** is correct because structured tool definitions let the model produce machine-readable arguments while the application remains in control of execution. **A** is incorrect because retrieved prose does not replace explicit tool contracts **B** is incorrect because fine-tuning does not give the application a controlled tool invocation protocol **C** is incorrect because plain-text tool suggestions are less reliable and harder to validate programmatically
questions.reference: [How to use function calling with Azure OpenAI in Microsoft Foundry Models - Microsoft Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/how-to/function-calling)

questions.stem: A project lead wants to use the managed platform capability that best supports **improve retrieval quality by producing coherent chunks during indexing**. What should the team choose?

options.text: Store each entire manual as one vector
Increase generation temperature during chat completion
Disable chunk overlap and metadata completely
Split source content on semantic or natural topic boundaries

questions.explanation: **D** is correct because coherent chunks improve match quality because each retrievable unit stays focused on one concept. **A** is incorrect because oversized chunks reduce precision and blur topics **B** is incorrect because generation temperature does not improve indexing quality **C** is incorrect because removing useful context and metadata can hurt retrieval quality

questions.stem: One team is building a RAG pipeline for a customer support system. Customer emails are the source documents. Select TWO preprocessing steps that are MOST important before embedding. (Select TWO)

options.text: Remove email headers (From:, To:, Subject:, Date:) and quoted reply chains to avoid embedding boilerplate and repeated content
Convert all text to uppercase for consistent tokenization
Replace customer names, email addresses, and account numbers with placeholder tokens to prevent PII from being indexed
Translate all non-English emails to English before embedding

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because email headers and quoted reply chains are noise — they do not contain useful semantic content and will degrade embedding quality and waste index space. Removing them ensures the embedding represents only substantive content.

**C** is correct because indexing PII exposes it to being surfaced in future LLM responses to other users, creating a serious privacy and compliance risk under GDPR and CCPA.

**B** is incorrect because converting to uppercase can hurt tokenization for modern subword tokenizers (like BPE or SentencePiece) and provides no quality benefit.

**D** is situationally useful but not universally necessary — multilingual embedding models can handle multiple languages, and translation introduces potential errors and loses nuance.

[Data preparation for RAG in Azure AI Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-studio/concepts/retrieval-augmented-generation)
questions.reference: [Retrieval augmented generation (RAG) and indexes in Microsoft Foundry - Microsoft Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-studio/concepts/retrieval-augmented-generation)

questions.stem: A project lead wants to use the managed platform capability that best supports **detect harmful prompts and generated content before returning it to end users**. What should the team choose?

options.text: Use Azure AI Content Safety and related safety controls
Rely only on temperature settings to make the model safer
Shorten the system prompt so the model has less room to answer
Disable retrieval so fewer tokens are processed

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because safety services are built to classify harmful content, apply thresholds, and support responsible AI mitigations. **A** is incorrect because prompt length is not a direct safety control **B** is incorrect because temperature affects randomness, not policy enforcement **C** is incorrect because retrieval volume does not replace content moderation

See [Microsoft documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/content-safety/overview) for the documented behavior.
questions.reference: [What is Azure AI Content Safety? - Azure AI services](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/content-safety/overview)

questions.stem: A deployment is underperforming because the wrong approach was chosen. If the real requirement is **ground a chat app on frequently updated private documents without retraining the model**, which correction is BEST?

options.text: Rely on the base model's pretraining knowledge only
Use Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Azure AI Search as the retrieval layer
Fine-tune the model every time source documents change
Paste the full document corpus into each chat request

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because RAG keeps knowledge outside model weights and retrieves current indexed content at inference time. **A** is incorrect because model pretraining does not contain organization-specific updates **C** is incorrect because fine-tuning is slower and costlier than updating a retrieval index for changing knowledge **D** is incorrect because large corpora exceed practical context limits and sharply increase cost

questions.stem: A deployment is underperforming because the wrong approach was chosen. If the real requirement is **improve retrieval quality by producing coherent chunks during indexing**, which correction is BEST?

options.text: Increase generation temperature during chat completion
Store each entire manual as one vector
Split source content on semantic or natural topic boundaries
Disable chunk overlap and metadata completely

questions.explanation: **C** is correct because coherent chunks improve match quality because each retrievable unit stays focused on one concept. **A** is incorrect because generation temperature does not improve indexing quality **B** is incorrect because oversized chunks reduce precision and blur topics **D** is incorrect because removing useful context and metadata can hurt retrieval quality

questions.stem: A project lead wants to use the managed platform capability that best supports **detect harmful prompts and generated content before returning it to end users**. What should the team choose?

options.text: Shorten the system prompt so the model has less room to answer
Rely only on temperature settings to make the model safer
Disable retrieval so fewer tokens are processed
Use Azure AI Content Safety and related safety controls

questions.explanation: **D** is correct because safety services are built to classify harmful content, apply thresholds, and support responsible AI mitigations. **A** is incorrect because prompt length is not a direct safety control **B** is incorrect because temperature affects randomness, not policy enforcement **C** is incorrect because retrieval volume does not replace content moderation
questions.reference: [What is Azure AI Content Safety? - Azure AI services](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/content-safety/overview)

questions.stem: A deployment is underperforming because the wrong approach was chosen. If the real requirement is **improve retrieval quality by producing coherent chunks during indexing**, which correction is BEST?

options.text: Store each entire manual as one vector
Disable chunk overlap and metadata completely
Split source content on semantic or natural topic boundaries
Increase generation temperature during chat completion

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because coherent chunks improve match quality because each retrievable unit stays focused on one concept. **A** is incorrect because generation temperature does not improve indexing quality **B** is incorrect because oversized chunks reduce precision and blur topics **D** is incorrect because removing useful context and metadata can hurt retrieval quality

See [Microsoft documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/vector-search-overview) for the documented behavior.

questions.stem: An Azure AI Search vector index is configured with approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search. An ML practitioner notices that some highly relevant documents are not being returned even though they exist in the index. What is the MOST likely explanation?

options.text: The documents were chunked with overlapping tokens, causing them to be deduplicated during indexing
ANN search trades recall for speed and may not return all true nearest neighbors; increasing the efSearch parameter or switching to exhaustive KNN may help
The embedding model used at query time differs from the one used at indexing time
Cosine similarity does not support documents longer than 512 tokens

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because approximate nearest neighbor algorithms (like HNSW) use graph-based shortcuts to achieve fast search at the cost of recall — they may miss some true nearest neighbors. Increasing the efSearch parameter explores more candidates and improves recall at the cost of latency.

**C** is also a real problem (mismatched embedding models would cause poor results), but a model mismatch would affect all queries uniformly, not just some documents selectively.

**A** is incorrect because chunk overlap does not cause deduplication during Azure AI Search indexing.

**D** is incorrect because cosine similarity has no document length limit; the 512-token limit applies to some embedding model inputs, not to the similarity metric itself.

[HNSW algorithm parameters in Azure AI Search](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/vector-search-how-to-create-index)

questions.stem: Which TWO statements are correct about **allow the model to answer with current order status from an internal system while minimizing hallucinated order details**? (Select TWO)

options.text: Expose a tool that looks up order data from the source system at runtime
Ask the model to invent the order data if the tool is unavailable
Give the model thousands of example order numbers in the system prompt
Validate tool arguments before executing the lookup

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because runtime lookup grounds the answer in current transactional data instead of model memory **D** is correct because argument validation helps prevent malformed or unsafe tool execution **B** is incorrect because invented transactional data is an unacceptable hallucination **C** is incorrect because examples do not provide current per-user order state and waste context
questions.reference: [How to use function calling with Azure OpenAI in Microsoft Foundry Models - Microsoft Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/how-to/function-calling)

questions.stem: An Azure AI developer is configuring an Azure AI Search vector index. Documents are updated continuously in a source data store, and the engineer wants index changes to propagate automatically. Which Azure AI Search indexer configuration supports this?

options.text: A push-model index where the application calls the Documents Index API on every update
A pull-model indexer scheduled to run on a recurring interval or triggered by a change-detection policy
A Flat L2 index with exhaustive nearest-neighbor search
An HNSW approximate nearest-neighbor index with no indexer

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Azure AI Search pull-model indexers can be scheduled or triggered by a change-detection policy on the data source, automatically propagating updates to the index without application code managing each write.

**A** is incorrect because a push-model index requires the application to explicitly call the REST API to add, update, or delete documents — there is no automatic sync from the source.

**C** is incorrect because L2 (Euclidean distance) describes a similarity metric for vector search, not an index synchronization mechanism.

**D** is incorrect because HNSW is a graph-based approximate nearest-neighbor algorithm, not a data synchronization strategy.

[Azure AI Search indexers overview](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/search-indexer-overview)

questions.stem: A multi-turn customer support bot stores the full conversation history in the prompt. After 20 turns, users report that the bot starts ignoring earlier parts of the conversation. What is the MOST likely cause and the best remediation?

options.text: The LLM temperature is too high; reduce it to 0.1 to improve coherence
The conversation history exceeds the model's context window; implement conversation summarization to compress earlier turns
The system prompt is too long; shorten it to leave room for conversation history
The embedding model cannot represent long conversations; switch to a larger embedding model

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because LLMs have a fixed context window, and as conversation history grows, earlier turns get truncated or the model's attention is diluted across too many tokens — causing it to effectively ignore early context. The standard mitigation is to summarize older turns into a running summary.

**A** is incorrect because temperature affects the creativity and randomness of responses, not the model's ability to recall prior context.

**C** is incorrect because while shortening the system prompt helps marginally, it does not address the root cause of unbounded history growth.

**D** is incorrect because embeddings are not involved in multi-turn conversation history management for a chat completion model.

[Conversation history management in Azure OpenAI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/how-to/chatgpt)

questions.stem: One team wants to build a customer support chatbot that answers questions about their product documentation. The documentation is updated weekly. Which approach is MOST appropriate for keeping the chatbot's knowledge current without retraining a model?

options.text: Use Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with a vector index refreshed from the updated documentation
Fine-tune an open-source LLM on the documentation corpus each week
Include the full documentation in every prompt to the LLM
Use a proprietary LLM with a large context window so no retrieval is needed

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because RAG decouples the knowledge base from the model weights, allowing the vector index to be refreshed with new documents without retraining. The retrieval component always fetches the latest indexed content at query time.

**B** is incorrect because fine-tuning weekly is expensive and slow, and fine-tuned knowledge can still become stale between retraining cycles.

**C** is incorrect because documentation corpora are typically too large to fit in any context window, and cost per query would be prohibitive.

**D** is incorrect because even large context windows cannot hold enterprise documentation corpora, and the model's parametric knowledge would still have a training cutoff date.

[Retrieval Augmented Generation with Azure AI Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-studio/concepts/retrieval-augmented-generation)
questions.reference: [Retrieval augmented generation (RAG) and indexes in Microsoft Foundry - Microsoft Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-studio/concepts/retrieval-augmented-generation)

questions.stem: Which TWO statements are correct about **allow the model to answer with current order status from an internal system while minimizing hallucinated order details**? (Select TWO)

options.text: Expose a tool that looks up order data from the source system at runtime
Ask the model to invent the order data if the tool is unavailable
Validate tool arguments before executing the lookup
Give the model thousands of example order numbers in the system prompt

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because runtime lookup grounds the answer in current transactional data instead of model memory **D** is correct because argument validation helps prevent malformed or unsafe tool execution **B** is incorrect because invented transactional data is an unacceptable hallucination **C** is incorrect because examples do not provide current per-user order state and waste context

See [Microsoft documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/how-to/function-calling) for the documented behavior.
questions.reference: [How to use function calling with Azure OpenAI in Microsoft Foundry Models - Microsoft Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/how-to/function-calling)

questions.stem: One team is planning a solution and needs to **combine keyword precision with semantic/vector recall for enterprise search**. Which service or design choice should they make?

options.text: Use hybrid search that combines text and vector ranking
Use only vector search with no lexical signal
Disable ranking and return documents in ingestion order
Use only BM25 keyword search

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because hybrid search is designed to merge lexical and vector signals so exact terms and semantic similarity both contribute. **B** is incorrect because vector-only retrieval can lose exact-term precision for identifiers or rare phrases **C** is incorrect because ingestion order does not reflect relevance **D** is incorrect because keyword-only retrieval can miss semantically relevant documents with different wording

questions.stem: Which statement BEST describes token budgeting in a production Azure AI Foundry agent?

options.text: You must account for system instructions, retrieved context, tool call results, and conversation history all competing within the model's context window limit
Only user messages count toward context length
Tokens matter only for cost, not for model context limits
Azure AI Foundry caching eliminates context window limits

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because all prompt components — system instructions, retrieved context, tool call inputs and outputs, and conversation history — consume capacity from the same context window. Effective token budgeting requires managing all of these together.

**B** is incorrect because system messages, assistant turns, tool results, and retrieved context all count toward the context window, not just user messages.

**C** is incorrect because exceeding the context window causes errors or truncation regardless of cost considerations.

**D** is incorrect because Azure AI Foundry's prompt caching (where available) reduces cost and latency but does not increase or remove the model's context window limit.

[Azure OpenAI token limits and context windows](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/concepts/models)

questions.stem: One team is evaluating embedding similarity metrics for a legal document search system. Documents range from 1,000 to 5,000 words. Which similarity metric is generally preferred for normalized embedding vectors and why?

options.text: L2 (Euclidean) distance, because it captures absolute positional differences between vectors
Dot product, because it is computationally cheaper than cosine similarity
Cosine similarity, because it measures the angle between vectors and is invariant to vector magnitude
Jaccard similarity, because it accounts for token overlap between document embeddings

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because cosine similarity measures the angular distance between vectors, making it invariant to the magnitude of the embedding. For text similarity, documents of different lengths can still be semantically close even if their raw vector magnitudes differ.

**B** is partially true that dot product is cheaper, and for unit-normalized vectors dot product equals cosine similarity — but when vectors are not normalized, dot product conflates magnitude with direction, making it less reliable.

**A** is incorrect because L2 distance is magnitude-sensitive and less appropriate for semantic text search, particularly when documents vary in length.

**D** is incorrect because Jaccard similarity applies to set-based representations (e.g., bag of words), not dense embedding vectors.

[Vector similarity in Azure AI Search](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/vector-search-ranking)

questions.stem: A deployment is underperforming because the wrong approach was chosen. If the real requirement is **organize models, data connections, and evaluation assets inside a managed Azure AI workspace**, which correction is BEST?

options.text: Use an Azure AI Foundry hub and project structure
Create a SQL database and place prompts there
Deploy everything from a local notebook without a shared project
Use only a standalone storage account

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because Foundry hubs and projects provide the managed workspace structure for models, connections, tracing, and evaluations. **B** is incorrect because a database is not the primary AI application management surface **C** is incorrect because local-only work does not provide the managed collaboration model expected in Foundry **D** is incorrect because storage alone does not provide AI project lifecycle capabilities

questions.stem: One team is integrating external tools into an Azure AI Foundry agent. Select TWO good practices. (Select TWO)

options.text: Validate tool inputs against the declared schema before executing the tool
Return clear, structured error information so the agent can recover or ask the user for clarification
Hide tool side effects from logs to reduce storage costs
Make every tool accept one unstructured free-text string as its only parameter

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because input validation against the declared schema prevents malformed arguments from reaching the underlying system and allows the agent to detect and recover from errors early.

**B** is correct because returning structured error messages (rather than opaque failures) allows the agent to understand what went wrong and either retry with corrected arguments or inform the user.

**C** is incorrect because hiding tool side effects from logs undermines observability and auditability, which are critical for debugging and compliance.

**D** is incorrect because accepting only a single free-text string removes the schema structure that helps the model call the tool correctly and makes input validation impossible.

[Function calling best practices in Azure OpenAI](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/how-to/function-calling)
questions.reference: [How to use function calling with Azure OpenAI in Microsoft Foundry Models - Microsoft Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/how-to/function-calling)

questions.stem: An engineer needs to split a large collection of Markdown documentation files into chunks using LangChain. The documentation uses headers (# H1, ## H2) to organize sections. Which text splitter is MOST appropriate for preserving section boundaries?

options.text: CharacterTextSplitter with separator='\n'
TokenTextSplitter that splits on the 512th token boundary
RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter with chunk_size=100
MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter that splits based on Markdown header hierarchy

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter understands the Markdown heading hierarchy and creates chunks that align with document sections, preserving the logical structure of the document so each chunk is a coherent section rather than an arbitrary character-count slice.

**A** is incorrect because splitting on newlines without understanding section boundaries creates inconsistently sized and semantically arbitrary chunks.

**B** is incorrect because splitting at fixed token boundaries has no awareness of Markdown structure or content organization.

**C** is incorrect because RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter splits on character count recursively without respecting Markdown header structure.

[Document loaders and text splitters](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-studio/concepts/retrieval-augmented-generation)
questions.reference: [Retrieval augmented generation (RAG) and indexes in Microsoft Foundry - Microsoft Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-studio/concepts/retrieval-augmented-generation)

questions.stem: An Azure AI developer creates a push-model (direct index) vector index in Azure AI Search. How does this index type differ from a pull-model (indexer-driven) index in terms of data management?

options.text: A push-model index automatically syncs with a source data store; a pull-model index requires manual API calls to update
A push-model index requires the application to explicitly upsert and delete documents via the REST API; a pull-model index uses an indexer that automatically tracks changes in the data source
A push-model index only supports exact search; a pull-model index supports approximate nearest neighbor search
A push-model index stores raw text; a pull-model index stores pre-computed embeddings

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct and accurately describes the two indexing models. A push-model index is managed by the application — you call the Documents Index API to upsert or delete individual documents, giving you fine-grained control. A pull-model index is driven by an indexer that periodically polls or is triggered to pull changes from the data source automatically.

**A** is the reverse of the correct description — it incorrectly attributes automatic sync to the push model.

**C** is incorrect because the search algorithm (ANN vs exact KNN) is configurable independently of whether you use push or pull indexing.

**D** is incorrect because both indexing models can store embeddings; the distinction is purely about who manages document updates.

[Azure AI Search indexing models: push vs pull](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/search-what-is-data-import)

options.text: Fine-tune an open-source LLM on the documentation corpus each week
Use a proprietary LLM with a large context window so no retrieval is needed
Include the full documentation in every prompt to the LLM
Use Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) with a vector index refreshed from the updated documentation

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because RAG decouples the knowledge base from the model weights, allowing the vector index to be refreshed with new documents without retraining. The retrieval component always fetches the latest indexed content at query time.

**B** is incorrect because fine-tuning weekly is expensive and slow, and fine-tuned knowledge can still become stale between retraining cycles.

**C** is incorrect because documentation corpora are typically too large to fit in any context window, and cost per query would be prohibitive.

**D** is incorrect because even large context windows cannot hold enterprise documentation corpora, and the model's parametric knowledge would still have a training cutoff date.

[Retrieval Augmented Generation with Azure AI Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-studio/concepts/retrieval-augmented-generation)
questions.reference: [Retrieval augmented generation (RAG) and indexes in Microsoft Foundry - Microsoft Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-studio/concepts/retrieval-augmented-generation)

questions.stem: An engineer is designing a RAG pipeline. After evaluating the system, they find that retrieved chunks are often irrelevant to the user's query. Which of the following changes to the chunking strategy is MOST likely to improve retrieval precision?

options.text: Increase chunk size from 128 tokens to 1024 tokens to capture more context
Reduce chunk overlap from 20 tokens to 0 tokens to avoid duplicate content
Switch from fixed-size chunking to semantic chunking that splits on natural topic boundaries
Increase the number of retrieved chunks (top-k) from 3 to 20

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because semantic chunking creates chunks that correspond to coherent topics, making each chunk more likely to match a specific type of query and improving retrieval precision.

**A** is incorrect because very large chunks reduce precision — a 1024-token chunk may contain many topics and will be retrieved for queries that only match a small portion of it.

**B** is incorrect because removing overlap can cause important context that spans chunk boundaries to be missed, hurting recall without reliably improving precision.

**D** is incorrect because increasing top-k adds noise by pulling in more potentially irrelevant chunks, hurting precision even if recall improves.

[Chunking strategies in Azure AI Search](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/vector-search-integrated-vectorization-ai-studio)
questions.reference: [Integrated Vectorization with Models from Microsoft Foundry - Azure AI Search](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/vector-search-integrated-vectorization-ai-studio)

options.text: Fine-tune the model instead of using structured tool definitions
Define tools/functions with JSON-schema-style parameters and execute them only when the model requests a tool call
Ask the model to describe the desired API call in plain text and let the user interpret it
Store tool instructions inside retrieved documents and hope the model follows them

questions.explanation:

See [Microsoft documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/how-to/function-calling) for the documented behavior.

questions.stem: An engineer is building a RAG pipeline and needs to extract text from a large collection of PDF files stored in Azure Blob Storage. Which Azure AI approach is MOST appropriate for processing these documents at scale?

options.text: Use a single-node Python script with PyPDF2 to extract text sequentially on a local machine
Store the PDFs directly in Azure AI Search using the Documents Index API without any extraction step
Use Azure AI Document Intelligence integrated with an Azure AI Search indexer skill set to extract and chunk text in parallel
Use Azure Machine Learning model serving to parse PDFs asynchronously via a REST endpoint

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because Azure AI Document Intelligence (formerly Form Recognizer) is purpose-built for extracting structured text from PDFs, and when combined with an Azure AI Search indexer skill set, it processes documents in parallel from Blob Storage at scale.

**A** is incorrect because a sequential single-node script will not scale to large document collections in a production pipeline.

**B** is incorrect because Azure AI Search cannot store raw binary PDFs as indexed documents; text extraction must happen before or during indexing.

**D** is incorrect because Azure ML model serving is designed for real-time inference, not bulk document processing pipelines.

[Azure AI Document Intelligence in Azure AI Search skill sets](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/cognitive-search-skill-document-intelligence-layout)

questions.stem: An engineer is configuring an Azure AI Search vector index and must choose between cosine similarity and dot product for their index. The embedding model they are using does NOT normalize output vectors to unit length. Which similarity metric is safer to use and why?

options.text: Dot product, because it is always faster to compute than cosine similarity
Cosine similarity, because it normalizes for vector magnitude, making the similarity score reflect only the angular (directional) similarity regardless of vector scale
L2 distance, because it accounts for both direction and magnitude and is always the most accurate
Dot product, because it gives higher scores to longer documents which typically contain more information

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because when embedding vectors are not unit-normalized, dot product scores are influenced by both direction and magnitude. Two vectors pointing in the same direction (same semantic meaning) will have different dot product scores if one has a larger magnitude — introducing a length or frequency bias. Cosine similarity divides by the product of the magnitudes, normalizing the score to reflect only the angular difference.

**A** is incorrect because the speed difference is negligible and is not a valid reason to compromise retrieval accuracy.

**C** is incorrect because L2 distance also captures magnitude differences and is generally less appropriate than cosine similarity for semantic text matching.

**D** is incorrect because preferring longer documents based on magnitude is a bias, not a feature — longer documents are not necessarily more relevant to a given query.

[Similarity metrics in Azure AI Search vector search](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/vector-search-ranking)

questions.stem: One team is reviewing design choices for **allow the model to answer with current order status from an internal system while minimizing hallucinated order details**. Which TWO choices are valid? (Select TWO)

options.text: Validate tool arguments before executing the lookup
Ask the model to invent the order data if the tool is unavailable
Give the model thousands of example order numbers in the system prompt
Expose a tool that looks up order data from the source system at runtime

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because runtime lookup grounds the answer in current transactional data instead of model memory **D** is correct because argument validation helps prevent malformed or unsafe tool execution **B** is incorrect because examples do not provide current per-user order state and waste context **C** is incorrect because invented transactional data is an unacceptable hallucination

See [Microsoft documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/how-to/function-calling) for the documented behavior.
questions.reference: [How to use function calling with Azure OpenAI in Microsoft Foundry Models - Microsoft Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-services/openai/how-to/function-calling)

questions.stem: One team is planning a solution and needs to **preserve important history in a long-running assistant conversation without overflowing the model context**. Which service or design choice should they make?

options.text: Append every previous message forever
Increase temperature to make the model remember more
Summarize older turns and keep the latest high-value details in context
Replace the system prompt with the conversation history

questions.explanation: **C** is correct because conversation summarization compresses older context while preserving salient facts within the available context window. **A** is incorrect because unbounded history eventually exceeds context limits **B** is incorrect because temperature does not expand context capacity **D** is incorrect because removing system guidance weakens control and still does not solve growth of history

questions.stem: One team discovers that their document corpus contains many near-duplicate product descriptions (slightly reworded versions of the same content). What is the BEST approach to handle this before indexing?

options.text: Index all near-duplicates to maximize recall; the vector search will score them similarly and the top result will still be correct
Use a larger chunk size to merge near-duplicate content into single chunks automatically
Apply MinHash LSH or embedding-based cosine similarity deduplication to identify and remove near-duplicate chunks before indexing
Increase the similarity threshold in vector search to filter out redundant results at query time

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because near-duplicates inflate the index unnecessarily, and at query time they consume multiple slots in the top-k results with essentially identical content, crowding out diverse relevant chunks. MinHash LSH is efficient for text deduplication at scale; embedding-based cosine similarity can catch semantic near-duplicates.

**A** is incorrect because while the top result may be correct, redundant chunks waste retrieval slots and reduce the diversity of context provided to the LLM.

**C** is incorrect because increasing the similarity threshold changes query-time filtering but does not eliminate redundancy from the index itself.

**D** is incorrect because larger chunk sizes do not merge semantically similar but textually non-adjacent documents.

[Data preparation for RAG in Azure AI Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-studio/concepts/retrieval-augmented-generation)
questions.reference: [Retrieval augmented generation (RAG) and indexes in Microsoft Foundry - Microsoft Foundry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/ai-studio/concepts/retrieval-augmented-generation)

questions.stem: Which option is MOST appropriate for the following requirement: **ground a chat app on frequently updated private documents without retraining the model**?

options.text: Paste the full document corpus into each chat request
Use Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Azure AI Search as the retrieval layer
Rely on the base model's pretraining knowledge only
Fine-tune the model every time source documents change

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because RAG keeps knowledge outside model weights and retrieves current indexed content at inference time. **A** is incorrect because model pretraining does not contain organization-specific updates **B** is incorrect because large corpora exceed practical context limits and sharply increase cost **C** is incorrect because fine-tuning is slower and costlier than updating a retrieval index for changing knowledge

See [Microsoft documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/vector-search-overview) for the documented behavior.

questions.stem: One team is planning a solution and needs to **improve retrieval quality by producing coherent chunks during indexing**. Which service or design choice should they make?

options.text: Store each entire manual as one vector
Split source content on semantic or natural topic boundaries
Increase generation temperature during chat completion
Disable chunk overlap and metadata completely

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because coherent chunks improve match quality because each retrievable unit stays focused on one concept. **A** is incorrect because oversized chunks reduce precision and blur topics **C** is incorrect because generation temperature does not improve indexing quality **D** is incorrect because removing useful context and metadata can hurt retrieval quality

questions.stem: A project lead wants to use the managed platform capability that best supports **ground a chat app on frequently updated private documents without retraining the model**. What should the team choose?

options.text: Use Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Azure AI Search as the retrieval layer
Paste the full document corpus into each chat request
Fine-tune the model every time source documents change
Rely on the base model's pretraining knowledge only

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because RAG keeps knowledge outside model weights and retrieves current indexed content at inference time. **B** is incorrect because large corpora exceed practical context limits and sharply increase cost **C** is incorrect because fine-tuning is slower and costlier than updating a retrieval index for changing knowledge **D** is incorrect because model pretraining does not contain organization-specific updates

questions.stem: Which option is MOST appropriate for the following requirement: **combine keyword precision with semantic/vector recall for enterprise search**?

options.text: Use only vector search with no lexical signal
Use hybrid search that combines text and vector ranking
Disable ranking and return documents in ingestion order
Use only BM25 keyword search

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because hybrid search is designed to merge lexical and vector signals so exact terms and semantic similarity both contribute. **A** is incorrect because vector-only retrieval can lose exact-term precision for identifiers or rare phrases **C** is incorrect because ingestion order does not reflect relevance **D** is incorrect because keyword-only retrieval can miss semantically relevant documents with different wording

questions.stem: Which option is MOST appropriate for the following requirement: **improve retrieval quality by producing coherent chunks during indexing**?

options.text: Split source content on semantic or natural topic boundaries
Disable chunk overlap and metadata completely
Store each entire manual as one vector
Increase generation temperature during chat completion

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because coherent chunks improve match quality because each retrievable unit stays focused on one concept. **A** is incorrect because generation temperature does not improve indexing quality **B** is incorrect because removing useful context and metadata can hurt retrieval quality **C** is incorrect because oversized chunks reduce precision and blur topics

See [Microsoft documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/vector-search-overview) for the documented behavior.

questions.stem: A retrieval system needs to balance semantic similarity with exact keyword matching for product codes. Select TWO approaches that BEST help. (Select TWO)

options.text: Use hybrid retrieval that combines dense vector search with sparse BM25 keyword search
Use a cross-encoder re-ranker to improve the final ordering of retrieved results
Remove all metadata fields from the index
Increase the model temperature during generation

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because hybrid retrieval combines dense vector search (semantic similarity) with sparse BM25 keyword search, ensuring exact token matches like product codes are found while also capturing semantic relevance.

**B** is correct because a cross-encoder re-ranker evaluates query-document pairs jointly and can significantly improve the final relevance ordering of retrieved results before they are passed to the LLM.

**C** is incorrect because removing metadata fields reduces the filtering and ranking signals available during search.

**D** is incorrect because generation temperature controls output diversity, not retrieval ranking quality.

[Hybrid search in Azure AI Search](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/search/hybrid-search-overview)
